CURRENT AFFAIRS.

In our last issue we had some notes on the Rathmines
Audit, the missing vouchers and Mr. King. We stated
that probably we would have more about it.  This
week we direct special attention to the article by Mr.
Michael Sullivan. Notwithstanding the apparent climb
down of the auditor, and the production of the missing
vouchers, it appears that even still only some of the
missing vouchers have been produced. A whole set of
vouchers covering over £2,000, it appears, are yeb
missing. The auditor, Mr. King, never gave a hint of
the fact that these vouchers were missing, and if the
ratepayers who have taken the trouble to look into
these affairs had not been stubborn men, had they been
people who could be put off with a suave official smile,
and had they taken only one day to investigate the
vouchers, they would most probably not have noticed
that even yet some of the vouchers are missing

'By the way, as we are referring to an L.G.B. auditor,
might we ask, why should not the L.G.B. be compelled
to recruit its staff of auditors from the ranks of such

- seasoned and experienced officials as, say, the clerks of

District Councils and Unions? Surely such experienced
officials would know a great deal more about these
accounts than men shovelled into auditors’ jobs.

A firm by the name of Messrs. Leverett and Fry,
Ltd., sent to our private address some touting matter
concerning two makes of jam—Southwell’s and Beach’s.
We presume that thése are British .made for British
We do not eat jam ourselves, but there are
several Leaderettes of whom the same cannot be said
by any means; but they always eat Irish jam for juve-
nile Irish-Trelanders. - We presume that Leverelt and
Fry, Ltd., live by trading with the Irish people. ~We
don’t object to them selling foreign jam if their cus-
tomers ask for it, but why do they not send out cir-

culars to our, and others, private addresses setting forth

e ‘particulars of Williams and Woods’ Dublin-made
jam, or the jam of any other Irish'manufacturer? See
what Leverett and Fry, Ltd., got for their ha’penny

~stamp and their circular fo our private address!

Some kind friend has sent us a poster issued by the
North Monaghan Divisional Executive of the United
Trish League. It is headed: ‘“ Irishmen support Irish-
This is no question of class or creed.””  The

men.
Poster goes on:—‘‘ Demand Irish-made goods. Make
your shop-keepers stock Irish-made goods. Don’t be

put off; insist on having the Irish-made article. Let
your food be Irish. TLet your clothing be Irish. Let
overything in your household, and every article you
use, be Irish. Remember charity begins at home,
therefore let Monaghan support Monaghan men and
their work first, then Irishmen and their work. We
neither want foreigners mor their work. (By order).
North Monaghan Divisional Executive, U.L.L.” That
is the right spirit, and we would be glad that every
Divisional ‘Executive of the U.L.L. did something in
the way of appealing directly to the populace to sup-
port Irish goods. The U.T.L., like Irish goods, must
stand the test of competition, and if it wants: to keep
vigorous and healthy, it must outbid any rival organi-
sation.  Competition may conceivably be the life of
politics as it is said to be the life of trade.

Fonritng is the name of a new system of shorthand
just published by Messrs. Sealy, Bryers and Walker,
and paper, printing and engraving are Irish, as is also
the author who conceals his identity under the initials
R. M. R. The book is probably the first of its kind
published in Dublin.  The author claims that
 \vithin a month, by ordinary diligence, any one should
be able to write it as quickly as ordinary longhand;
and in from three to six months, according to individual
talent and the amount of exercise, it is possible to
acquire sufficient speed to report an ordinary speaker.’””
A well-known man in the Irish Language Movement is
engaged in adopting Fonritng to Irish.

Athenry has had notoriety thrust upon it. During
recent times some wanton fellows spiked a meadow of
the Department; there was a strike, the end of which
we never definiately heard ; there was a blackguardly and
dastardly attack on a man returning from Mass with
his mother, some five or six miles from the town; there
was a bit of cattle-driving there, and the notorious Lord
Ashtown does not live far away. Athenry is, and has
been, infested with extra police. At the time we were
there we were informed that the cost of the extra police
was £240 a week, and half that fell on the unfor- -
tunate ratepayers.

There is a paper called the ‘‘ Loughrea Guardian.’’
We don’t exactly know what its politics are if it has
any, but a motto under the title suggests that it lives:
“To right the wrong—to requite the right.”  Of
course we can take all that with as much salt as suits
our taste. The mottoes of some local papers would
almost tempt saints to jealousy. There has been some
interesting correspondence and information in recent
issues about the situation in Athenry.

The piling up of the rates for extra police must be
a serious matter for a struggling, straggling, non-in-
dustrial town like Athenry. Why don’t the ratepayers
call a meeting, and discuss the question, and decide on
some course of action? A letter in the ““ Loughrea
Guardian ’ states:—‘‘ We have an average of forty
extra police for over twelve months, with two transport
cars. They have never brought one solitary successful
prosecution, simply because there was nothing to pro-
secute, for all the agitation that was carried on was
kept within the law by old and seasoned politicians who

not want turmoil, and have nothing to gain by
- notoriety.” Now, why are the police there if they can-
- not institute a successful prosecution? Well, we are
not ““ knowledgable >’ enough to answer that question.
There are, according to the letter we refer to, three
police barracks—Nos. 1, 2 and 3; No. 2 pays £18 per
~ month, at the rate of £216 a year. Barrack No. 3 is
Ist at nearly £300 a year!

- We know that some ‘“ patriotic’’ towns petition for
‘ soldiers in their midst. - Now an extra body of police-
men; with healthy appetites and, no doubt, keen thirsts,
are material considerations for many people in Athenry.

‘Why are the police authorities paying at the rate of
£216 and £300 respectively for two houses in Athenry?
This question certainly requires attention. In_ view of
the anxiety for having Tommy Atkins in various towns
it would be well if arrangements were made that extra
police in a district should be so housed and fed that the
district would not make a penny out of their presence;
so long as some influential people batten on extra police
there is a temptation to keep them there, %

Notwithstanding the notoriety that has been thrust
ou Athenry, and the extra police that have been
dumped into it, the police don’t appear to have done
much forthe money they cost,

Whatever may be thought of cattle-driving, there can
be only one opinion of the characteristic British unscru-
pulousness, so far as Ireland is concerned, with which it
i3 received and cooked in England, where the Potteries
and West Ham are. Some months ago the British rail-
way workers had a grievance, or thought they had—we
did not follow the case closely; at the present time a
certain section of the Irish agricultural population has
a grievance. A lot of the land is given over to cattle,
and the animals who are not worth so much per 1b. on
foot, but who only have immortal souls, are pushed away
on the mountain sides or huddled in the agricultural
slums. The British railway men threatened to strike,
and by doing so threatened to dislocate the trade and
industry and life of the three kingdoms; the TIvish
people with the grievance give the cattle walking exer-
cise by way of demonstrating their grievance. In the
Britishers’ case a minister of the Crown went in‘o con-
ference with a view to settle the case, and avert the
threatened strike to dislocate the trade and indus‘ry of
the three countries, and he was hailed as a great man
when he fixed up a working settlement; in the Irish-
men’s case there is an attempt being made to bully the
minister concerned into adopting coercion; Ireland is
held up as an outrageous country by screeching British
hypocrites, who in private, are probably—a large num-
ber of them—Ileading disgraceful lives. It is the right
of the Briton to threaten to strike and dislocate the
nation’s trade; but some of the picus Britishers turn
another eye at Paddy if he demonstrates his grievance
perhaps not wisely, but too well. Many Britishers in
the sweet Potteries district, no doubt, are in a state of
righteous indignation at the sins of Ireland!

However, though we point out all this we are aware
that the Briton can play this game with success, and
that state of things has to be faced. The British hypo-
‘crite can always raise up an anti-Irish scare when he

Some kind friend has sent us a page of a paper called
the “ Meath Chronicle’”” of November 30th. On the
page we see without acknowledgment an article,
“Trinity College Agriculture ’—by ““J.M.,”” that
appeared originally in the LeapEr of November 23rd.
Now provincial papers are quite welcome to lift any
article from the Lrzaper so long as they acknowledge
the source from which they take it, but it is a mean
thing to take an article out of a paper, and print it
without acknowledgment,

Mr. William TField, M.P., has written as follows to
Mr. Birrell, M.P., re the imported stone scandal for
th> College of Science :—*‘ Dear Sir,—I understand that
the tender for the new College of Science building,
amounting to £110,000, has been almost accepted,
although of this amount £40,000 is for imported Port-
Jand stene. Tf this be a correct statement, it means
labour taken away frem native quarrymen and stone
cutters. There is plenty of more suitable durable Trish
stone available, as proved by the Kingsbridge Terminus,
St. Paul’s Church, Arran Quay; the New Museum,
Kildare Street, whereas the Portland Stone which was
introduced for carved work in front of T.C.D., is
partially crumbling away. In view of repeated ques-
tions, replies and implied promises in the House, cor-
respondence between the Board of Works and the Dublin
Industrial Development Association and with the Lord
Mayor, I have also written to the Board, pending
their answer, I have to ask that you will enquire into
the terms of this contract. The Irish taxpayers claim
that a fair ratio of expenditure should be retained in
this country.”” We wonder what will the eloquent Mr.
Birrell say to that. The eloquent Mr. B. has been
making a lot of speeches recently which we have not
read, but we will read with interest hix reply to Mr.
Field’s letter.

at the last Munster Feis in Cork, is progressing by leaps
and bounds. The annual subscription is only half a

'._l‘he lxeglect on the part of the Primary Schools of
?_[l'elzmd. with rigard to agricultural and manufacturing
industries, was one of the important matters dealt with
at the recent Industrial Conference at Limerick. The
.‘I"llgh Sheriff of Limerick, Mr. E. J. Long, remarked :—

\:Ve should follow the lead of Germany and America,
which adapted their Primary education to the needs of
the country. Gur Primary education system was about
the worst in Europe. It was designed for the purpose
of turning out non-productive citizens; it was not de-
signed for the purpose of turning out citizens who would
have to work with their hands and eyes.”” By this time
nearly every intelligent man in the country, we should
say, is fully alive to the nation-killing trend of all our
education, Primary, Intermediate, and such University
education as we have. But the pcople have not enough
power to give effect to their views. If an ounce of
power creates a certain effect in a normal self-governing
country, in Ireland in crder to create a similar effect,
you would require to bring about a ton of pressure, a
few brass bands, some more or less disorderly meetings,
a few panes of broken glass, and the risk of an unlimited
number of persons being sent to jail for a few months,
you would require to bring all that to bear in order
to preduce a siwilar cffect. And people get tired of
cxerting all this huge power to create such a little effect,
and they become apathetic, and ‘‘ intelligent ’’ English-
men see as clearly as daylight that Paddy lacks self-
reliance. What we lack in relation to all these things
Clerks, or people fit to be nothing
but clerks, are turned out by the thousand in this
country, and they may flow out of the country looking for
jobs, while even the clerkships in the country, to some
extent, are rather exclusive preserves and are not open
to free competition. The Christian Brothers’ boys
could clerk all the banks in Ireland, if they had a
chance, but they don’t get the chance.

An interesting Christmas Number is that of ‘‘ The
Lady of the House,”’ the great feature of which is ‘“ In
the Days cof the Irish Volunteers,’” by Crawford Hart-
nell, which deals with a stirring period of Irish history.
The two presentation plates are ‘‘The Muster of the
TIrish Volunteers in College Green,’’ painted in 1779 by
F. W. Wheatley, R.A. This is the companion picture
to Wheatley’s ““ Irish House of Commons in 1780, dis-
covered in England, and issued for the first time last
Christmas by the ‘“ Lady of the House.”” . The second
presentation plate with this year’s number is “The
Northern Volunteers Celebrating the Fall of the Bastile
in the old High Street of Belfast in 1793,”’ painted by
John Carey. Mr. Hartnell’s article is embellished with a
wealth of rare illustrations, including ‘‘ The Defeat of

Thurot’s Fleet after the landing at Carrickfergus,”
‘‘ The Irish Volunteers drilling on the lawn at Leinster
House, Dublin,”” ‘ The Cradle of Irish Liberty,”” ‘ The
Old Presbyterian Church of the Volunteers at Dun-
gannon,”” where the famous convention was held on 15th
February, 1732; ‘“ Charlemont House, Dublin,’”” where
James, first Earl of Charlemont, Henry Grattan and
Ienry Flood drew up the three chief resolutions for
submission to the Dungannon Convention, etc.
The prize frame design competition for a series
of designs by Irish artists for picture frames to be
executed in Irish woods by the woodworkers of Kilkenny,
ferm a most attractive feature of this Christmas Number,
The price of the Double Number is 1s., published by
Messrs. Wilson, Hartnell and Co., Commercial Build-
ings, Dublin.

We have received from Messrs. E. Smithwick and
Sons, Ltd., an illustrated pamphlet containing the report,
of Sir Charles Cameron on their Irish-made mineral
waters, cordials, hop bitters, etc., which, we are glad to
note, shows that these Kilkenny-made articles can com-
pete in any market. We also note that Messrs. Smith-
wick and Sons give direct employment to the large
number of 150 hands in Kilkenny.

We see that the Coon is returning. He made his
appearance in the Workmen’s Club, 41 York Street,
Dublin. A Coon by the name of Power sang ‘‘ The Old
Kentucky Home ;”’ another Coon sang ‘‘ Carry Me Back
to Old Virginny;’’ another sang ‘‘ Little Yaller Girl.””
Mr. P. Whelan sang ‘‘ Lonesome Coon.”” We read cf .
‘“ Messrs. Mac and M‘Carthy, cross-talk comedians and
dancers, whose patter kept the house in roars of
laughter.”” We thought that 41 York Street was in
Dublin, and not in Alabama.

When purchasing goods as a result of advertisements
in the LEADER, our readers would do this paper a service
by mentioning it as the source ci their information.
We have ample reason to know that many already do
so, but in our large circle of readers there must be
many others who omit to mention the name of the
Leaper, when doing business with firms as a result of
advertisements in 1's columns. Now that we have
suggested that references to the LEADER in this connec-
tion would be serviceable to it, we are sure they will
avail of such opportunities in future.

At the annual general meeting of Messrs. A. and R.
Thwaites and Co., the Chairman, Mr. A. W. Orr, in the
ccurse of his remarks, said :—‘‘ Last year he had alluded
to the Exhibition; and he then hoped it would have a
good effect on their sales; but he thought that business
people generally had had a sad awakening over the Exhi-
bition. Te was not going to say whether it was the
fault of the management or of the weather. They
expended a large amount on the Exhibition, and they
had to pay a large sum for the concession to allow them
to sell their mineral waters.  But, unfortunately for
them, they could not get their soda water when they
asked for it at the Exhibition, and they had to complain
to some of the gentlemen who got up the project. The
money they (Thwaites, Ltd.) got from their sales at the
Exhibition was a good deal less than what the business
cost.””  The above is taken from the report of the
““Irish Times;”’ the report in the ‘“Freeman’’ also
contains Mr. Orr’s references to the Exhibition, and we -

The removal of Messrs. James Duffy and Co., Ltd.,
from Wellington Quay is like the removal of a land-
mark from that part of Dublin. However, Duffys are
a progressive firm, and we congratulat? then} on
their transfer to the more central and smta})lc site of
Westmoreland St. We wish the .ﬁrm continued pro-
sperity in their splendid new premises.

We have received three recent publications of M@Ssrs.
Maunsel and Co., Abbey St., one or more of which we
hope to have reviewed at an early da.tp. .They comprise
«he Northern Iron,”’ by George Birmingham; ‘“ The
T4in,”” an Irish epic told in English verse, by Mary A.
Hutton, and a collection of short stories and sketches by
Stephen Gwynn.

A splendid chromo-lithograph is given this”yeax" as
usual as a supplement with the ‘‘ Irish Rosary ” Christ-
mas Number. The subject of the picture is the Holy
Family, reproduced from an old Ttalian painting, and
apart from its artistic value has a great devotional
aspect.  The interesting figure of the ‘‘ Blessed Cure
D’Ars ”’ is the subject of an article by the Countess de
Courson. Amongst other contributions to the Christ-
mas Number of our contemporary is the ‘“ Abbey of
Melvose,”” ‘“ Cat-keeping as a Profitable I-[obb;’," and an
illustrated story, “ The Viking’s Captive Maid.”

THE POLITICAL TANGLFJ

EACE and good-will is a hackneyed phrase in a
P Christmas Number, but certainly it cannot be
said that either good-will or peace is a hackneyed quality
in Treland. Treland, because she is not progressing as
much as she ought, and because she has not sufficient
power over her own destiny, is a rather short-tempered
country; so many people see how things might be
righted, and no one having much power to do anything
we are prone to quarrelling whenever we take suffi-
cient interest in public affairs even to quarrel about
them. Tt is impossible to keep up ‘‘ movements’’ that
don’t move before the public all the time; the public
gets wearied of them. In a self-governing country a
successful movement becomes part of the law of the
land; in Treland creates great
commotion and enthusiasm, and does not become part
of the law; it then begins to drag and dwindle and bore
people.  Of many movements in TIreland it may be
said—as a witty friend of ours said of men engaged in
literature in this country—they may have a past, but
no future. Even wrangling between parties palls after a
while, and peace, if in its turn it is not going to lead
us very far afield, is at least a change. There is a
wish for peace in Ireland at the present time which is

many a movemeny

largely a reaction against the futile wrangling of our
various politicians and political fishwives.  People
would naturally like to put a “ comforter ** in screech-
ing William’s mouth, or give him ‘ Unity,”” give him
buns, cakes, sugar-candy, anything he asks for, if he
would only stop his awful screeching, or at least tone
it down. He is irritating people, irritating people in
their turn to screech and counter-screech. Of course,
‘“ Unity ’ is not a policy, and it may be a fool’s para-
dise; but if a working arrangement is made up between
Mr. Redmond, Mr. Healy, Mr. O’Brien, Mr. Dillon,
and their several followers before Christmas the country.
will be thankful. In due time, no doubt, fresh troubles
will brew, but a truce to the wrangling, even for a year
or two, would be a relief, and might give some cohesion
to political effort in Ireland. At present matters are
very much in a state of chaos, and Mr. Larry Ginnell
appears to be de facto the political leader of the Irish
race at home for the moment; and though we feel sure
that Mr. Ginnell is sincere, we would not like to have
our money in a concern of which he was managing
director.

The ‘‘ITungarian’’ fiasco is fizzling out. . Tt has
neither sense nor men behind it. It has not borne
fruit, though it is some years old now; it has no%
created a mercantile marine, nor even an extra bargs
on the Grand Canal; there is no system of arbitration
courts, and no scheme for crippling England has been
put in operation; Mr. Martyn has won the fight for
the restitution of his club rights in Kildare Street;
Mr. Griffith has received some money as a result of his
appeal to' the ‘British law courts, and Mr. Sweetman is
ready to receive the British income gatherer when

2 calls. The poor ‘ Hungarian ’’ fiasco is like a penny

balloon going off with a squeak. The ‘‘ Hungarians

~are not facts, they are fancies, and they may be prac-

tically left out of calculation. In the mere political

world—a section, and a rather exaggerated section, of

- Irish life—the men who count are Mr. John Redmond,

-Mr: Healy, Mr. O’Brien, Mr. Dillon, and their respec-

tive followers. If they should ‘‘unite’’ it may be

necessary to keep them, and the country also, alive to

the fact that politicians are not the monopolisers of

nationality, for if William is given “ Unity ’’ in order

to stop his screeches, he would probably want to. put

out nearly all lights but his own political bonfire. Tt

ought to be possible to get the: unruly team in harness,

and we hope that at this Christmas time it may come

to pass. But wait till they commence to trot! Wait

until William lets a few yells out of him, and Tim

kicks up his hind legs! Bystanders may then wonder

whether or not there is to be a runaway and a smash

up. However, let us not anticipate the worst.

5 It is easy to talk of Boer Home Rule, but where are

our Bothas, and De Wets, and burghers? The strength

-and tactics of the Irish political party were tested, and

the kicking out of the Council Bill was the ultimate

result. It was, before the Bill, and at the kicking out

of the Bill, a bungled business. We must make the

best of a bad job, and for peace sake let O’Brien stop

screeching, and let Redmond make a working agree-

ment with the author of the ‘‘ Diary” if he can, and

“let the combination attempy to get a University, or an

amending Land Bill, or a parish pump Bill—let them

get anything, for the country must make up its mind

to take politically what the politicians can get, and

keep on bungling and muddling for more. At present

the politicians are getting nothing; they are wrangling,

distracting and disgusting the country, and are shaking

the confidence of the people greatly to the detriment of

the progress of the industrial and intellectual revival.

The politicians’ sphere is at best a small one in the

economy of the nation, and that is all the more reason

that the jarring factions should cease their wrangling,

and, in the spirit of peace and good-will, drop their
quarrels, and combine on some workable programme.

EADERS of the LEADER will remember Kickham’s
description of ‘“The Carrick man and his dog,
* Trueboy.”” Walking through the streets of the town on
a fine day you may see scores of the type—at the street
corners and in the alleys—‘‘no occupation’’ being
written as plainly all over them as if they were labelled.
Carrick-on-Suir is situated in the centre of an excep-
tionally fertile district, fourteen miles from both Clon-
mel and Waterford, and can be looked into from Slieve-
namon and the Comeraghs. It is an important market
town, the surrounding district being extensive and fer-
tile, and the farms not too large; there are no grazing
ranches, and there is much tillage.  Flat-bo‘tomed
boats and small steamers, laden on the return journey
only, ply continually between Carrick and Waterford.
Altogether, the town is so situated as to be, possibly,
something more than a distributing station for foreign
goods. .
Carrick was at one time one of the.most prosperous
towns in the South, with its cotton and woollen factories
~ and saw-mills; now it is fast decaying; the mills are
closed down, and there is absolutely nothing being done
in the way of production. O’Neill Daunt, in his ‘‘ Ire-
land and Her Agitators,”” mentions that the cotton
industry of Carrick supported over 4,000 of the popula-
tion. The saw-mills gave employment to nearly one
hundred, and these had probably four hundred others
dependent on them.
There is an old saying, that ‘‘ wherever you go you'll
- meet a Carrick man.”” No wonder; when the industries

IRELAND AT WORK.

A DUBLIN JAM AND SWEET FACTORY.
SPENT upwards of two hours in the factory, or
factories, of Messrs. Williams and Woods last
Thursday evening. And I saw so much and learned so
much that I don’t know where to begin. I am as fulf
of facts as a small boy would be full in another way if
he were allowed to roam without restraint through a jam
and sweet factory.  Mr. Barber showed me over the
place. One of the principal workers, certainly we
should say the heaviest, is Mr. Ward. He has been
in charge of the firm’s Sugar-boiling Department for
almost forty years; he is an artist in sweets. He
was working on a large American, or rather Irish-
American order for ‘“ Rock,” when, in company with
Mr. Barber, I reached his section of the factory.
“Rock!”” the very mention of that to me, now almost
obsolete term, takes me hack, as probably it will take
thousands of my readers back in a similar manner, o
the time when I was a small boy of about nine years
of age, peeping in with other boys on our way to
school to the shop where rock was being made in a
primitive manner, and where we bought brandy balls,
twenty-four a penny, if my memory serves me correctly.
But it is many years ago since I was nine, and the arf
of sweet-making has advanced.

Mr. Ward made a spscial line of ““rock ”” whilst I
was looking on, a most interested spectator. He was
making letters of the alphabet, but he went about his
work in such a quick yet easy way, that I had doubts
as to how the result would come out. I did not know
what he was at. What was my surprise when the whole
thing was done, and the ‘ rock > was made, to find that
wherever you broke the white ‘‘ rock,”” you were offered
in clear-cut red lettering, as sound and wholesome a
bit of advice as ever was offered in this country. The
advice was: ‘‘ Read the Leaper.”” Mr. Ward is evi-
dently more than an artist in sweets!

Messrs. Williams and Woods are one of the largest
jam and sweet manufacturers in Ireland—they alss
make pickles, sauce, candied peel and preserve fruits
whole in bottles, and they employ nearly 400 hands. Itis
a big Dublin factory, and when you have gone through it
and have seen the neat, cheerful workers in the various
departments, you might well ask yourself, what is
there in the cry so common these days that Treland is
not fitted for factories and that sort of thing? This
firm does not find Dublin unsuited for factory work, and
though they employ close on 400 hands, they could
employ more if the country ceased importing so muck
jam and sweets and gave the business to them. The out-
put of the factories is, we understand, on the increase
from year to year ; and the increase has been most marked
during the past seven or eight years, since the time
when the LeADER first made thousands enthusiastic sup-
porters of Irish industry and clamourers for Irish goods.

The extent of the premises is bewildering ; there are
zeally two factories, one composed of various buildings
having a frontage to Great Britain Street, and the
oﬁherz connected by an overhead bridge with the original
bu}ld}ng, has a frontage to Loftus Lane. In the latter
building the jam is made. Great Britain Street was
once a place of fashion, and in oné of the houses that
comprise the factory, the Duke of Wellington’s father
was probably born; anyway, he lived there. Of course,
it is not suggested that the firm expects anyone to pay
extra for their sweets on that account! Several old
mantel-pieces were sold some time ago on an average of
about ~ £80 apiece, and one of the ceilings
partly cut up to facilitate the working of
machinery, is a class of ceiling that is not put up now
-in Aylesbury road, not to talk of Great Britain Street.
The first part of the factory I saw was a.lozenge de-
partment.  Special lozenges for an American order were
being made. Conversation lozenges are inscribed with
fitting mottoes and messages in three languages, Irish,
Welsh and English. There is an indescribable amount
of mz}chinery all over the place, but several of the
machines have rollers, and remind one of paper-making
machinery. One of the machines mixes, kneads, rolls,
and stamps lozenges, the waste is dropped down in
Teceivers, and is put back into what I may call the
sausage machine again. Other lozenges are made by
hand. Sweets are made in starch moulds, others in
Tudia rubber moulds; some sweets are cut by hand,
and others by machinery. They have the most up-to-
date sugar-boiling plant, capable of turning out an
enormous quantity of sweets daily. Sugar is certainly a
versatile ingredient, for it plays many parts. Molten
sugar goes into moulds, and when it cools it comes out
in the form of pigs—curiously enough their chief market
for sugar pigs is Scotland. In the Department where
the French and German style of confectionery is made
some high-class sweets composed of marzipan are pro-
duced to represent various flowers, fruits, vegetables,
etc. French nougat caramels are very extensively made
in this department. There are two or three thousand
names for swee's produced at the factory, so the reader
will be obliged to me for not enumerating them all.
Some of them are registered. Irish toffee, retailed at
1d. a stick, also put up in a neat 3d. package contain-
ing six different flavours, is a popular line; penny
bundles of butter scotch, 6d. bottles of sweets, 6d.
enamelled tins of sweets, and so on, and so on. A new
line the firm have just introduced will be sold as Woods’
exhibition toffee at 8d. per Ib. A strong permanent
line is the penny Diamond Prize Packet. This packet,
is well known to juveniles in the three Kingdoms.
Prize packets come and prize packets go, but the Dia-
mond goes on gaily all the time. It is now over thirty
years on the market, and is still going strong.
Santa Claus Stockings, full of all sorts of toys and
things, are made to retail at 3d., 6d., Is., 2s., and
2s. 6d. Unfortunately, the toys, etc., are not made in
TIreland, yet it is something to have the sweets miade
here, and the putting together of these Chrisumas
stockings keeps many hands busy.

Tt is time now to say something of the jam making.
Nearly all the fruit used is Irish grown; sometimes
when there is a scarcity, a little has to be imported.
The jam factory has its frontage on Loftus Lane. Tts
jmmense steel girders are a particular feature of the
structure. The floor where the jam is_ I_)oxled is
concreted, and as a heavy stock has to be carried, there
is need for.a big system of steel girders to stand the
strain. On cne floor alone there are from 700 to 800
tons of jam stcred. The jam factory is a roomy, airy

COMPENDIUM.

BODKIN, MATTHIAS McDONNELL, Judge,
Journalist, and Jester.—Eminent also as: a litterateur
and Corinthian.  Connected with ‘“ United Iroland ”’
in the later ecighties, he played no small part in the
overthrow of Parnell. Member of Parliament for some
years, he considerably enhanced the reputation of
Gladstone by his brilliant eulogies of him under the
pseudonym of ‘A Young Parliamentary Hand.”
Leader writer on the ‘“Freeman’s Journal’ for
many years. The extremely intimate knowledge of
criminal life displayed in his fictional writings, notably
““Paul Beck, Detective,”” attracted the attention of
the Irish Executive, with the result that he was ap-
pointed County Court Judge of Clare in 1907, Clare
being at that time a notorious criminal centre. It is
not true that he was not previously a member of
the Trish Bar, as has been erroneously stated.

CAMERON, SIR CHARLES, Public Health Officer
and.City Analyst.—With a view to exercising some con-
trol over the dietary of his fellow citizens, and also to
provide an example of the benefits of simple and plain
food, he instituted a Dining Club called the Corinthians,
where a course of Hygienic dinners were given. These
dinners were open to the public on the payment of a
moderate fee. A special feature of the scheme was the
providing of free dinners to actors, authors and other
artistic persons of slender means. As an aid to diges-
tion, after-dinner speaking was largely indulged in at
these entertainments.  The city death-rate declined
rapidly as a result of the efforts of this philanthropic
society.

CROSSLEY, W. F., Pioneer and Explorer.—Pene-
trated into the wilds of Ireland and carried civilization
to hitherto barbarous districts. Discovered Killarney,
Greystones, and Killaloe. Edited ‘“The Irish
Tourist,”” a paper which exercised almost as great an
influence on the Ireland of his day as ‘ The Nation ”’
of Davis and Gavan Duffy. Intrcduced the use of soap
and water to Irish Hotels.

ESMONDE, SIR THOMAS, usually known as

" ““Silken Thomas,”” or ‘‘ The Knight of the Party.”—
Acted as Chief Whip of the Irish Parliamentary Party
for many years, but became addicted to Sinn Fein in
1907 and gradually succumbed.

HEALY, TIMOTHY MICHAEL, eminent Politi-
cian, Lawyer, and Wit, who made History, Money, and
Enemies resvectively in these several capacities.—Origi-
nally heia s.iong views on the Irish Qustion, but even-
tually accepted the position of Humourist to the House
of Commons—a poorly-paid but greatly coveted post.
Had numerous aliases—' The Man in the Gap.” ‘ The
Poisoned Bullef,”” and ““ The Splendid Calamity.” Was
at all times strongly in favour of Unity among Irish
Politicians. : L

HYDE, DR. DOUGLAS, distinguished Linguist.—
Introduced the use of Irish into Ireland. Leader of the
Trish (Ireland) People at home and abroad. Visited
America and collected a large sum of money for the
Gaelic League. After hard study, obtained complete
mastery over the English language, and spoke it almost
as fluently as his native tongue. United the two great
forces of Talking and Walking by a series of Processions
in Dublin, conducted on Life-boat principles. ~Was
honoured by the Royal University, but recovered.

MARTYN, EDWARD (“‘the VIIL.””), Lord Protec-
tor of the National Commonwealth, and Ez'n'l ol:' Kl}d{xre
Street.—A. considerable authority on music, his dislike
of certain anthems brought him into disfavour with the
adherents of another school. Accompanied by a host of
Sham Fenians (or Sinn Feinians), He assailed' the'citadel

~ in Kildare Street and completely captured it. Of him

the famous mot was made: ‘ If all Kildare Street cannot

rule this man, then let him rule all Kildare Street.”
The fortifications are still shown which were erected to

resist his entry. IHis dramatic work, ““The Hqa.bher-
field,”” showed that if his lot had not been cast in the
stormy avena of politics, he might have won a reputa-
tion as a man of letters. %

MEATH, REGINALD BLABBAZON, EARL OF,
Statesman, Educationist; and Philanthropist.—In-
vented Empire Day and Daisies. Was the first man of
his day to perceive the educational value of the police-
man, and to extend the principle of the cane in the
Preparatory School to that of the baton in the Uniyer-
sity. In the same spirit promoted the Police-aided
Children’s Clothing Society, and the Police-aided Uni-
versity Degree Conferring Association. The latter
scheme did not, however, prove popular, and owing
to an organic breakdown, the Earl withdrew from
University life.

MOORE, GEORGE, distinguished Novelist.—An
admirer of Art, he painted his hall door green, but he
objected to the music of barrel organs which caused him
to become unpopular in Italy, and ultimately led to a
breach' with Rome. Left Ireland in 1905 and settled
down in Ely Place.

O’BRIEN, PETER, Lord Chief Justice.—Reduced
Jury Packing to a science, and Jury trials to a humbug.
Originally prominently identified with the popular
party, his sense of what was due to himself rescued him
from error. Ranks with O’Connell, O’Gorman Mahon,
Keogh and Bodkin among the heroes of the Clare
people.

O’BRIEN, WILLIAM, distinguished Politician,
sometimes known as William the Silent.—Originally a
Parnellite; became a quick-change artiste in 1891.
Retired into private life about 1895. Emerged 1897
and founded United Irish League. Assisted in blowing
the New Era Bubble in 1903. ~Retired into private life
a little later. Emerged again to unmask his former col-
leagues; was partial to conferences and strongly in
favour of Unity among Irish Politicians. Author of
‘“ When we were Boys,”” and ‘ The Tragedy of 1903.”*
Though not a Sinn Feiner, appears to have embraced the
dcctrine of abstention from the House of Commons.

O’CONNOR, T. P., the greatest Popular Leader in
Ireland since Parnell.—A journalist of considerable
talent, he started a number of papers in England in
which he carried on a violent campaign on behalf of Irish
freedom. These journals, circulating among all classes
of the community, could not fail to produce a' very great
and beneficial effect on the fortunes of the Irish cause.
The ardour with which he conducted his political labours
made it necessary for Mr. O’Connor to reside in London
so as to escape danger of arrest, and he even represented
an English constituency. Nevertheless, with singular
audacity, he visited Ireland for one or two days each
year. To the sagacity of his counsels may, in no small
measure, be attributed the extraordinary state of Irish
politics in 1907.

PLUNKETT, RT. HON. HORACE, Social Re-
former.—Invented Co-operation, Self-Reliance, The
Department of Agriculture, and other useful customs
and institutions. Published ‘ Ireland in the New Cen-
tury ** in 1904, which proved so popular that he was
able shortly afterwards to retire from official life. Was
considerably assisted in doing so by the Irish Party.

REDMOND, JOHN E., Politician and Statesman.—
Leader of the Parnellite Minority from 1891 to 1900,
then elected chairman of the entive Ivish Party. An
eloquent speaker but reserved in judgment, so that his
real views on most subjects are unknown. Is under-
stood to have been opposed to Sinn Fein. His sensitive
appreciation of the people’s wishes was admirably dis-
played in connection with the Council Bill, also his
spirit of self-effacement. Was strongly in favour of Unity
among Irish Politicians.

SYNGE, J. M., Explorer and Dramatist.—Discovered
the Aran Islands. Was a captive in these barbarous
regions for some ‘years till rescued by Yeats (g.v.).
Described his adventures in a series of plays which gave
rise to some controversy at the time of their production.

CITY AND TOWN IMPROVEMENT.

l ) subject that has yet been discussed in the

LEADER seems to me likely to be fertile of good, as

- this matter of town improvement which just lately has
been brought into the foreground.

People of good taste and tolerably high ideals do not
hestitate to criticise and condemn what displeases them
in the regions of art, literature and music, but the com-
position of a town seems to be regulated without
criticism -or guidance. The barbaric builder, or
ground-speculator, or the well-meaning but absolutely
tasteless person, seems to have it all his own way with-
out interruption or remark, not only in our few big
cities, but in the towns and villages throughout the
country. Certain powers devolve on local authorities,
but to whom are our Councillors to look for guidance in
the exercise of those powers? In the buildings of
Churches, Schools, and Parochial Halls the clergy
would seem to have a great deal in their power, but
circumstances control the best laid schemes, and the
man with most money and best intention has not

s always the best taste. o

% There is great need for public interest to be
wakered and popular taste educated, and this can be
well done by arousing feelings of loyalty and pride in
the towns and villages we live in. The feeling of Civic
loyalty is in fact a sub-section of patriotism, the virtue
on which every Irishman prides himself. But how can
he love Ireland with pride, if he has to avow that he
| lives in a mean and dilapidated town, which he has
e made no effort to improve? Effort is within the scope
of every one. Many a country town that I know is
beautified by the taste of some simple cottagers at the
it town end, whose golden thatch and snowy whitewash,
I . and seat under a fuschia bush, or mallow plant by a
| green door gives proof to the passer by that here are

people who value brightness and light and air.

In my opinion the great need in most of our middle-
sized country towns is some space right in the middle
of the town where the aged or the idlers could sit and
loiter and chat.

Corner boys, as they are called, make a miserable
spectacle leaning and standing against a house-wall,
and doubtless they would be unwelcome if they con-
gregated anywhere save at the public-house; but in the
Scripture parable the corner boys of our age are repre-
sented by ‘ the children sitting in the Market Place,”

2 where they are at ease and picturesque. Take the great
= bare square at Mitchelstown for example. ~ What a
~ difference would be made in it by a group of trees, and
_substantial stone-built seats on which you might sit and

smoke, if needs be, and enjoy the pure air blowing down
~ from the Galtees, or have the sun blazing down on you
with summer fervour in the month of March, and your
seats so constructed as to cut off the bitterness of the
east wind. With a given amount of sitting-out accom-
modation, each little town could be its own Sanatorium.
To all constituted authorities who will be awakened to
a sense of duty to their towns by your towns-improve-
- ment campaign, let me recommend a book, which is not
for the thousand since it costs exactly one guinea.

I know of mo other publication so calculated to in-
spire ideas as to beautifying streets, and public parks,
open_spaces, and historic ruins. This is a book pub-
lished and written by Professor Patrick Geddes, of
Edinburgh, who was applied to to make recommenda-
tions as to how the ancient Scotch Burgh of Dunferm-
line might best benefit by a munificent Carnegie dona-
tion for its improvement. 5 .

Turning over the pages and inspecting the hundreds
of illustratious, I see every minute something that sug-
gests a possible change in some Irish town. There are
ideas here, as to how to change everything for the
botter, a big public building, a hum-d'rum street or
torrace of mecan houses, a road-bordering fence, an
ancient ruirn, a bit of river-bank, a pond.

The possible improvements are illustrated by the art
of photography. We are given the photo of the place
as it is. Then the artist’s aid is called in and certain
simple additions are painted into the picture. Even a
slight alteration at times produces a marvellous effect ;
and the reproduction of the photo thus “ faked * (to use
a technical term) shows exactly what the place would
look like. By artistic argument of this sort it would be
casy to convinee obstinate Urban Councillors. Our
little seaside resorts in the midst of glorious scenery are
too frequently blots on the face of nature. And the
builders go on building without any idea that they are
contributing to destroy the future of their town, for to
be popular, it should be a pretty place. The hotels,
above all, should aim at having verandahs and sitting-
out accommodation, and a view from the windows of
the scenery which people stay there to see.

Professor Geddes’ book offers an education on this
subject, and should be acquired for all our public
libraries and studied by all our public-spirited young
men and women who intend to cultivate the spirit of
Civic loyalty, and to help in the uplifting of Treland by
beautifying the towns.

I should make it plain that Professor Geddes in all
his suggestions pays greal attention to the matter of
preserving relics of local history and giving them fitting
surroundings. Seeing how he deals with Dunfermline
with its memories of Scottish kings, one would like to
propose to him as problems the cases of Kilmallock, or
of Drogheda, or indeed of certain parts of Dublin City,
where the neighbourhood of dingy tenements obliterates
many a historic site. -Derry offers wonderful possibili-
ties, and Newry, looking so picturesque at a distance,
should be made more pleasant to walk in. Castlewellan,
near the Mourne mountains in Co. Down, offers a de-
lightful pattern for a little town, with its great rows of
trees down the streets, each tree base walled around at
a height affording a comfortable seat in the shade. I
remember arriving there one late autumn evening after
dusk, when the dry leaves were drifting and rustling
all over the streets and footpaths, and the people were
busy with brooms keeping them out of the shop doors.
Castlecomer, in Co. Kilkenny, is another place which
offers to me a beautiful memory picture on a frosty
winter day. Strokestown, in the West, and Ballina-
screen, in (}'o. Derry (some call it Draperstown), are
wide and high and airy. But what ugly towns; there
are scores of them in every province. I won’t mention
any of them, only I will conclude, Mr. Editor, by saying
Lha_t there will be all the more credit to those who try
to improve and beautify them. Some of the crookedest,

. queerest towns, morcover, that neither you nor I would

admire from an architectural point of view, are to be
admired on account of the social character of the
inhabitants.

Professor Geddes’ book can bo got from the publisher
at The Outlook Tower, Castle Hill, Edinburgh, and I
hope to hear of some of your City and my City libraries
acquiring it. Tts title is— A Study in City Develop-
ment. H AvricE MILLIGAN.

K HIGHLAND DANCE.

) A REGULAR  Glasgow : evening—foggy, drizzly,
| muddy—a crowd of shivering humanity hanging
- outside the St. Andrew’s Hall, straining their necks to

- catch a faint glimpse of a happier world—such was my

. first impression of the ball got up by the Highland
- Association.
But _my second impression—a stream of beautiful
% ladles_m brightly-coloured dresses, each with a tartan
szgsh tied slantwise across her breast, gentlemen in kilts,
dirks, sporans, and all the other accoutrements of a full
Highland costume, wending their way past some lady
of high rank (T forget to inquire what her name was) to
whom they bow, past the city dignitaries ‘o whom they
also bow, into a large waiting-room, where they form

themselves into couples preparatory to the Grand
March into the spacious dancing-hall.
This is a society function. Rarls, countesses,

baronets—scions of, more or less, ancient and noble
families from not ouly Scotland, but even England and
Ireland, ave to be found here; while the titular heads
ci the Highland clans are present almost to a man. In
the beginning of the procession the couples take prece-
dence according to their rank—a circumstance involving
many problems giving rise, I am told, to much petty
heartburning in the breasts of the mighty. Towards
the end of the procession comes the writer of this
article, who has been fortunate enough to have as his
partner the lady to whose enthusiasm, energy, and long-
suffering this and many other of the arrangements con-
nected with the Feill are chiefly due. Should this
article fall into her hands (which is unlikely unless the
writer of it takes the liberty of finding out her address
and sending it to her), let it be the means of expressing
his thanks for the kindness which she showed in looking
after a lone stranger, in giving him all the information
he asked, and in pulling him thro’ his first reel—but of
this more later on.

She begins the conversation.  “ The worst of you
Irish Gaelic Leaguers,” she says, ‘“is that you are all
Radicals.”” An extraordinary remark this may sound
to those who have never been out of the Green Isle.
Not to me, however, who have recently become quite
used to hearing Nationalism called Socialism, and to
being told that the ‘Irish Leaguers’ (Gaelic
Leaguers?) ‘‘ had that strike or something in Belfast.””
Discovering that she refers to the Nationalist Party in
Parliament (who are unfortunately the very antithesis
of Gaelic Leaguers), I pointed out how Isaac Butt, Par-
nell—not to speak of such rebels as John Mitchel —were
ardent Conservatives (I hope I was right, Mr. Editor,
but not being an editor myself, I am fallible like other
human beings).

We are now in the ball-room, and the moise of the
pipes prevents coherent argument. The procession
forms all kinds of snake-like contortions, with a mathe-
‘matical precision bordering on the miraculous. At
times T am separated from my partner, and again I find
myself by her side. Finally, we pass through an arck-

 way of lances held by ten members of the Imperial Army,
the procession breaks up, and the first item of the pro-
gramme is at an end.

The next event is a four-hand reel. The whole room
is dancing, and I am prancing like a bear, in earnest
endeavours to copy the other three, who belong to my
set. I have never danced a reel before, but I get
through without treading on anyone’s toes.  Let me
here state that I think it might possibly be a good thing
if Scotch dancing were introduced into Ireland. It
weuld be much better to turn to a Scotch reel when one
is tired of one’s own native dances than the Kitchen
T.ancers or the Boston Waltz, for a Scotch reel combines
the skilful step of the jig and with all the romp and
abandon of your up-to-date importations.
 The reel is over, and Herr Iff’s band strikes up a
waltz. The Celtic glamour is dispersed! Even if it is
impossible to do without the waltz just for one evening,
are there no Scottish bands good enough to play for this
éGlite assembly? Yes! The Celtic glamour is dispersed ;
ut a dance ’s a dance for a’ that, and the ovening

ﬂ‘;:gggggs errily along with a multitude of partners proud

THE LEADER. .

of their Gaelic names, proud of their Gaelic lineage,
but (with one or two exceptions) unable to speak ome
word of their own native tongue. I carelessly ‘“ put my
foot in it’’ by asking one fair daughter of.the Camp-
bells if her clan fought for Prince Charlie, but gef
myself into favour again by evincing a genuine interest
in the genealogy of the family, whose name she derives
from Campus Bellus (the beautiful camp), I suppose
because she considered the generally accepted derivation
Cam Beal (crooked mouth) too unromantic.

There are, of course, many here who are not Gaels.
One of my partners comes from Edinburgh, and she is
ontertaining in her description of the difference bevween
the society where she livez and that of Glasgow. TIn the
capital everyone is tco good for everyone else, and
snobbery reigns supreme. In Glasgow it is not the case
that ““ butter looks down on bacon.”” A man starts asa
grocer in the back street. He gradually rises to the
dignity of a corner shop in a crowded thoroughfare,
then he becomes a universal provider, and finally a
retired merchant with a fine house in the West End.
But he is never ashamed of his humble origin, nor do
people look down on him because of it. I am just inftro-
ducing her to the meaning of our expressive word
““seonin *’ when the band suddenly starts to play the
next dance, bringing our chatter to an abrupt termina-
tion.

I again meet my first partner. She is on the look-out
for me in order to introduce me to a fellow-countryman,
a Nationalist M.P. I summon up all my Irish, and
prepare to tackle him, and to brand him as a shoneen
should he be unable to answer me. I inwardly hope
that such should be the case, for the worst of a beginner
addressing anyone in Irish is that he is apt to get
answered in the same tongue. But when we had
arrived at where we had supposed him to be, he had
vanished ; so one of us was rescued from annihilation in
a linguistic duel.

More waltzes, lancers, two-steps interspersed with
reels, and the night is gliding swiftly by, when suddenly
in the midst of the whirl of excitement I catch the musi-
cal accents of two speakers conversing in Gaelic. Of all
the seven or eight hundred Gaels present, among whom
were to be found the heads of numerous ancient families,
these two old men were -the only ones whom I heard
talking in the language of their ancestors.

And now there is confusion abroad. No one seems
to know who his or her partner is. However, the
explanation is soon passed round. A couple or more
dances have been missed out in order that the ball may
not drag on too late. For to-morrow there is work to
be done. Thirty stalls have to be decorated for the
great Highland Bazaar, the Feill a’ Chomuinn Ghaid-
healaich, which is going to raise £10,000 to promote the
teaching of Gaelic in the Highlands. Of old, the Kilted
Scot came, claymore in hand to persuade the Lowlander
to give up some of his superfluous wealth. Now he
comes armed with cushion and Harris tweed, and the
gentler persuasion of a good object is going to induce
Craesus of Ghlaschu (always generous when his heart is
touched) to unloose his purse strings.

The dance is over, and that the departing guests may
be speeded, the lights are one by one turned off all over
the hall. Again T am in the street. It isstill foggy and
muddy, but the drizzle has ceased, and the mass of
shivering humanity has given place to a host of four-
wheeled cabs, in which its more fortunate brethren
are whisked home to their downy couches.

The mere Irishman idly watched the coming passen-
gers. This tall man in gaiters and shooting-jacket,
striding down the platform with an air of responsibility
and dignified aloofness, is the County R.M. After
him a sharp-faced individual in swaggering coat-tails
bustles up to the station-master with some indignant
query; he is a local magnate of no little influence; for
has he not been to America, from which he has re-
turned with a wife and a fortune and a wealth of
maxims borrowed from the gospel of dollardom. He
strolls down the platform, condescending to an occasional
acknowledgment of a chorus of salutes from rustics,
whose hands, in spite of Labourers’ and Land Purchase
Acts, are still rather ready to raise their caps. There
is a little fuss at the door before it opens to admit a
small dandified person, neatly got up in blue uniform
with silver facings, a perfumed cigarette under his fair
moustache.  This is the local D.I. He throws away
the end of his cigarette and joins the magistrate, both
walking up and down apart from the general crowd.
The mere Irishman is beginning to feel bored, when
his attention is roused by a stir outside the station door,
caused by the volcanic approach of a huge red motor
car. The car sweeps up with an insolent ‘‘ponk,
- ponk,”’ past the group of jarveys and donkey-drivers
clustered round the door. It is Lady So-and-so, of
Castle Blurneyem, leaving for the London season, after
a tiresome two-months of golf and motor-pic-nic and
county ‘‘ bear-party,’’ carried out, though perfunctorily,
with the éclat of an oriental potentate on his progress
through the more outlying and uncivilised provinces of
his empire. My Lady descends from her car, and undu-
lates in a cloud of frills and fiounces (all English, except
for one touch of Carrickmacross lace), up to where an
obsequious lackey is looking to her boxes. Lucky, as
My Lady passes on, is the village minister’s wife if she
receives a condescending smile from this pattern of
Anglo-Trish aristocracy ; and lucky beyond all bounds if
the great person deign so far as to stop for a few
gracious words, while at a respectful distance the dis-
pensary doctor’s lady looks on in a transport of envy
and admiration. At some distance up the platform two
little boys, the great lady’s nephews, are ready to start
to school—Harrow, probably. They are well wrapped
up from the atmosphere of the town, and in spite of their
charming youthfulness, have already caught the correct
manner—a kind of feudal overlordliness—with which to
address the porter and other Irish people.

The mere Irishman, feeling too modern to be able to
appreciate such feudalism, shifts his seat to a place
where he is out of view of the platform, and falls a-won-
dering as to the strange all-pervading serpent’s trail
from which even the Railway Company in Ireland can-
not escape. Ie muses vaguely upon the far-seen possi-
bility of the creation in Ireland of a genuine home-born
basis of social ‘values. Tt is a difficult and not very
fruitful problem, so he lights a cigarette of home-grown
tobacco, and relapses into his reverie.  As the smoke-
clouds rise about him they shut out the platform and
the crowd, police-official, red motor-car and giande
madame, and there rises on his vision a state where
classes need no, longer be mutually exclusive and
mutually destructive, but ranked in ordercd,.hu.rmouy.
" He forgets for the moment that, in Ireland, the struggle
for existence is still at that primitive stage when class

In the paper when stress was laid on the roast-beef
and plum-pudding of the typical English Christmas, my
mind went seeking for other English customs of a less
material nature associated also with Christmas; and it
fastened on the carol singing of the Waits. Now that
custom is a subject for laughter; we know the Waits
from comic illustrated English papers and from pictorial
post-cards, as misguided creatures who vie with the mid-
night cats in making night hideous, and whose unre-
hearsed performances always end in unrehearsed farce.
Until I read Hardy’s ‘“ Under the Greenwood Tree’’
this comic conception of the Waits was what came to the
surface whenever that term was mentioned in my hear-

Washington Irving. Hardy’s novel is perhaps the most
pleasantly-tempered book that has come from the hands
of any living English novelist. Almost without plot,
dealing with a very circumscribed range of human life,
with homely scenes and people, the book is for all so
dainty, so tenderly imagined, as to be fittingly com-
pared with these lovely old glees they were able to make
in the England of the sixteenth century. If I had read
the book before my mind had become so centred on Ire-
land as it has these latter years, I might perhaps have
enjoyed it better; I would not have shut the book with
*‘ How different !’ (for that is what must strike anyone

tale with the peasants of our own land); but then would
I have read it so well. It is essentially, as its name
implies, a tale of quiet English homes, ‘‘ dewy pastures,
dewy trees.”” It lets one know how an English poet
could come to make that grand phrase: ‘‘a haunt of
ancient Peace.”” Altogether a type of book one would
wish to see coming out of Ireland. But what I have just

etc.—itself is coated thick with the cobwebs and dust
of centuries. Snugly situated underneath sloping hills,
it folded its arms 300 years ago, and went to sleep. It
wakens into existence just occasionally—when the fleet,
comes in. It rubs its eyes; it~ shakes itself up; it
‘“fétes "’ the tars; it heaves a mighty yawn—and sleeps
again. Tor Bantry, you must know, is a seaside town.
"Tis more. ’Tis a naval port. And thereby hangs a
tale. That's the key to Bantry. Perhaps it is well for
Bantry that the stay of the fleet in its waters is not
perennial.  Were it so, the town would surely die of
forced activity. Tts sleep has been too deep. Its hiber-
nating existence has been too long; and, like all animals
that sleep the winter hours away, when the heat of
spring thrills the blood and throbs the pulse, the heart’s
action is for some time weak. Were too much activity
then displayed fatal consequences might ensue. Well,
Bantry hibernates. It wakens from its torpid state—
when the fleet comes in. It casts its skin; it changes
its coat; it decks itself gay; it courts the sea-men; 1t
invites them to dance and ball; it plays hockey and
Rugby with them; it drinks their punch; it picks up
their vocabulary—in fine, the dear old dreamy town:
does the part of mother to the boys; it takes them into-
its arms, and encircles them in a loving embrace.

But, when the fleet goes out? Ye gods, what change:
is here! Tears—such as angels weep—come down. The
broken heart, the heavy sigh, the sunken cheek, the.
empty till—all tell their tale. Gone are the colours.
gay and fair; gone the pleasant face, the happy smile,
the shining lights of eyes that love! Deserted are its
streets now, or almost so. But for a week some little
spark of life remains. “The latest slang from Plymouth.
music-halls and English sea-ports must get due expres-
sion. The corner crowds—those gentle fellows destined:
from early youth to keep up walls ‘‘gratis and for-
nought *’—practise their newly-acquired vocabulary.
Talk to a Bantry man now—of the weather, the price cf
coal, the impending railway strike, the political outlook,
or Sinn Fein—'twill all lead on (avoid it as you may)-
to the mystic query—*‘‘ Are we down-hearted?’ You
scarce have time to take it in—when from around’
corners, taken up by passers-by, passed along from
street to street, from lane to lane, from lip to lip—
comes sharply back the answer—‘‘ Not a bit of it !”’

As T said, this goes on for just a week. Then Bantry
sleeps again. No noise, now, to jar upon the ear. No-
foot-fall on the dirty streets—save the low, dull, heavy
thud of the policeman’s ‘hiloe,”” or the rumbling-
rattle of the country cart that by some mistake or
other has found its way to town. ’Tis no longer life—
mere existence—tolerable only to one who, like Milton’s:
Satan, could feel that:—

““The mind is its own place, and in itself
Can make a Ieaven of Hell, a ITell of Heaven!””

But Bantry is not dead; she merely sleepeth,  The
townspeople must attend to their * work.”” Their prin-
cipal business is standing—arms akimbo—at the doors,
and minding everybody’s business but their own.
Market day now is but a name, a memory of times and
things that were. The surrounding country is dotted
all over with shops, which supply all the requirements of.
the peasants, and relieve them of their eggs and butter
week by week. Farmers or their wives come in but
rarely now—chiefly on fair days. The publican gathers
in some of their spare money. The ““ Seag n Saop's,”
“Cheap Jacks ”’—those travelling dealers in shoddy
and cast-offs—get the rest. But has not Bantry got
some industries? What of Copithorne’s Woollen Mills?
Yes, she has got just that—but nothing more—nothing
in the manufacturing line, I mean. As far as I can
see, her principal industry is weaving, and on foggy
mornings the delicate fabrics spun by her hardy artisans
(the spiders) may be seen displayed on every bush. As
for the rest, beyond that—

““ Nothing to break life’s monotony, rippling it o’er
with its breath;

Nothing but dulness and. lethargy, weariness, sorrow,.
and death!”

Among the innumerable works of charity abounding in

human heart more powerfully that that which receives the
dying into its arms, cheers, strengthens, and brightens

saving many homes from the danger of infection.

Cross, to support which they appeal to a generous public.

“TAGOAT.”

X It is simply a
1 country parish situated in the Barony of Forth
in the County of Wexford. This parish is full of hap-
penings and interest. An enterprising Trish-Trelander
might write a fair-sized volume on Tagcat and name
it, ;ust}y, ““ First Steps to Nationhood.”” Cleanliness,
thrift, industry, and self-reliance are no mere idle terms
there. They are concrete facts in the every-day lives of

- the pzople.

Of the many things which struck me in Tagoat, T
shall give first place to the ‘“ Cottage Gardening Compe-
tition,’” it is so unique and the results are so wonderful.
It was started some four years ago by the parish priest,
Very Rev. Canon Doyle. Prizes to the amount of five
sovereigns are offered each year for. the best-kept cottage
and l?est~cx11§,i\vated garden (labourer’s)—one of two
sovereigns, two of one sovereign each, and two of ten
shillings each. The conditions are that the whole half-
acre is under tillage, and that a variety of vegetables,
fruit and flowers are planted; for the “ House Beauti-
ful >’ enters largely into this competition. Marks are
awarded to the number of 120—10 for each kind of the
vegetables, 10 for fruit trees, and 30 for fences, gates,
flowers, and general neatness inside and outside of the
cottages. To receive a prize, 70 marks must be gained
out.of the total. There is a handicap of 10 marks from
a winner of a first prize, and 5 marks from the winner
of a second prize in a previous year.

The first year only three or four were induced to
enter, and, when the day for judging came around, they
had a characteristic Irish objection to inspection,
especially the interviors of their homes. On that occa-
sion, the judge, Professor Carroll, was sent down by
the Department. Mr. Galvin, proprietor of the Wex-
ford, Rathdrum and Roscommon nurseries, has since
acted in that capacity.

The first shyness quickly wore off, and now, nearly.all
the labourers in Tagoat take the keenest interest in the
competition. The rivalry is very great, and each exer-
cises his wits to outshine all the others, not so much
for the hope of gain as because a ‘‘real home’’ in
preference to a shelter by the way-side and wholesome
appetizing food in lieu of a meagre diet of white bread
and black tea are becoming a necessity—things which
make life worth living.

These are mere bald facts conveying little. To be
appreciated at its full worth, the result of the Tagoat
Gardening Competition must be seen. Every garden is
a wonderland of vegetable produce, while the cottages
are bowers of beauty, cleanliness and health. So
quickly has a desire for that virtue, which is next to
Godliness, spread that you find a combined cook-house
and store-rcom built at the back of many cottages so
that the kitchen might be kept as a sccond-best parlour.
The best parlour is a model of daintiness. which many
more pretentious folk might copy with advantage—
linoleum on the floor, varnished wood cope-ceiling, the
walls papered and adorned with pictures, brackets and
nick-nacks, lace-trimmed blinds and lace hangings on
the windows, while home-made rugs, crochet antima-
cassars, and useful and ornamental articles in the car-
pentry line give the room a look of cheerfu]ness_and cosy
comfort. All the walls inside the cottages, including
the kitchen, are papered, the ceilings uPsLnirs plastqred
and papered, and those down stairs either cope-ceiled
with varnished wood or calico stretched across and white-
washed., There is usually a cement floor in the kitchen,

~ and, in all the other rooms, they are scrubbed almost

white when not covered with linoleum. Flowers
abound everywhere cither in pots or vases, and the f resh
air of heaven blows in always through the ever-opened
windows, minimising the risk of infection and doing
gallant work against that mortal foe of human kind—
consumption.  [n the parish of Tagoat, T saw over
thirty cottages, eack emulating the other in cleanliness,
neatness and adornment. Most of the carpentry-work
and masonry is done by the labourers in their off-time—
some get up at 5 o’clock in the summer mornings—and
nearly all the household things are made by the women.
Their rag rugs, patchwork quilts, crochet-work, ete.,

no mean addition to a labourer’s wages.

bad debts. Tagoat has also a splendid Parochial Hall,
built by ‘“Self-reliance.”” Tt is 60 feet long, 25 feet
in width, seats 300 people (more at a crush), has afine

5 on either side and a large hanging-lamp in the centre.
A house-to-house collection was made by the young men,
which, together with a few concerts and a drawing of

prizes, resulted in  £200 and freed the hall from debt.

Plano was purchased with the Anti-Emigration prize
of '€25’. offered by Sir Horace Plunkett, tl'lnd WOll] by
the parish 9f Tagoat. The hall is not merely an orna-
ment. It is continually being used for concerts, lan-
terp—entertaxpn\exlts, teas, prize-giving, and all sorts of
social gatherings, so that mental stagnation is impossible
in Tagoat where young and old are considered by their
indefatigable pastor.

Many other societies flourish in Tagoat—the Poultry
QO-operapxve Society, the Tobacco-growers’ Co-opera-
tive Society, both registered, and all profits divided
amongst the members, and the Forth Agricultural and

g Indust_rml Society from which all the others flow. This
latter s an unregistered Society in which the farmers of
the district assemble monthly to discuss all matters
(religion and politics excluded) of interest to the people
of _the Barony, to wit, roads, obstacles to fisheries, legis-
lation referring to land, and all grievances which affect
the people of the locality. This society provides,
through the County Council, for all kinds of lectures
under the Department Schemes, and runs the Forth
Show annually. It gets a subsidy of £10 only for this
latter from the Department. The Department sub-
sidised the tobacco-growing experiment (which has a
drying station in Tagoat parish) and the poultry-
fattening scheme, and paid all expenses of the fruit-
growing experiment at the instigation of the Agricul-
tural’and Industrial Societies. Tt had to be sought on
these matters. It did not go around looking for some-
thing to help or improve as some people fondly imagine.
Thus, without any special productive qualities in its
soil, has Tagoat forged ahead in less than half a decade
with energy, industry and perseverance. Naturally,
the farmers will not lag behind their workmen, and,
save in a few instances, have kept forging ahead, too;
their homes are surrounded with flowers, and neatness
and cleanliness have the place of honour. Tagoat is
lacking in many of the natural beauties one sees around
Ireland, but the perseverance of its people have amply
supplied the defects of nature. It is a fair land, beauti-
fied by the industry of man; and only lacks one thing
to make it a choice spot of the new Irish nation. The
Language Revival has not yet touched the heart of
Tagoat. The people are descendants of Anglo-Norman
" families who never have spoken Gaelic, if we except a
patois—half Gaelic, half Anglo-Saxon—which seems to
have survived even as far down as the beginning of the
eighteenth century. Not that they have any unwilling-
ness to studying Gaelic. The lack of teachers and the
difficulty in procuring them have been the stumbling-
blocks. If the ¢po-Timeipe or County Timeine took
the matter up, I am sure Tagoat would fall into line as
quickly as it has done in other good works
The genius of Tagoat, the Very Rev. Canon Doyle,
does not like adjectives, therefore, T will respect his
wishes, and conclude by saying to all priests and
. interested public men throughout Treland, read the 37th
verse of the 10th chapter of St. Luke; read and act on
it, and Ireland will be in very truth first flower of the
earth and first gem of the sea cre another decade has

passed over our heads.
Nora F. DEGIDON.

“ Can piety the discord heal,
Or staunch the death-feud’s enmity ;
Can Christian love, can patriot zeal,
Can love of blessed Charity?”

As the holy season of Christmas approaches, in whick:
the hearts of men all the world o’er show a tendency,
less or more, to melt into sweet and affectionate con-
sideration for one another before the divine message of’
““Glory to God in the highest, and on carth, peace to
men of good will,”” the time is opportune to refer to a-
phase of Irish social life of vast importance—social har-
mony and Christian feeling, or the second of the two
great commandments, love of the neighbour.

In the school books, which, in recent years, have-
received so much necessary criticism and improvement,.
there was a little poem when T was a school boy. It.
began thus:—

““ Whatever brawls disturb the street,
There should be peace at home;
Where sisters dwell and brothers meet,

Quarrels should never come.’’

T need not tell the reader that my acquaintance with:
educational places was very slight. Perhaps I might
digress for a moment to tell him of the two seats of
learning I attended. Well, in the first one we used to:
warm ourselves by sliding on the floor in the winter.
This was soon replaced by a more substantial structure,.
with a teacher’s residence adjoining.

The last time I revisited that hallowed spot, whick
I cannot approach without a choking heart and full
eyes—emotions of rapture and sad reflections combined
—the very hawthorn speaking, as it were, endearingly
of the happy days of childhood, on my way to the old
home, where the sun came peeping in at morn, I passed
in on the little -wicket and lifted the school-door latch,
expecting, as of yore, to find the dear old master there,
but just as I peeped in a sturdy ass popped out . his
head from behind the master’s desk, and, eyeing me:
keenly, protested against my intrusion in a long and
uproarious bawl. I turned away nothing eased at
heart. ~ Where are the bright spirits of that happy
long ago? They are gone. Yes, ‘“all gone from the
sunny hill. But the bird and the blue fly rove o’er it
still, the waters leap and the fresh winds blow,””

To return to my subject proper, the families who live
quietly within themselves, seldom quarrel with their
neighbours. The quantity of litigation and disputation
which prevails in the Green Isle is, I submit, most
abnormal and out of all proportion to the population
and the needs of the people. Does this indicate that
we are doing our duty in preserving and handing down
to our children undiminished the great legacy of St.
Patrick, so well described by Sir William Butler as
“ the most beautiful branch- that has ever grown upon
the great tree of Truth?’ The reader will be relieved
when I say the political quarrels form no part of my
theme, though, sometimes, indirectly, they may be an
element in the question. I refer to the wanton and
cruel attacks, and counter attacks the people—almost:
always the poorer portion of the population—make on
one another from year to year in the inferior Courts of
Law. In tho last year recorded, 1905, there were 58,350
civil prosecutions initiated in those Courts by the

7] HUE newspaper reports of the local Police Court.
proceedings always interest me. Whether it is-
that from them one gets such glimpses of the failings of
poor human nature, or whether it is that in that part
of England wherein I am a present dweller, the-
offenders are often Irish or of Irish extraction, I do
not know, but the fact remains that the Police Court:
news is interesting. James McCarthy has been up for
beating his wife or neglecting his children ; John O’Con-
nell has been drunk and disorderly; Mary Ann Casey
has been disturbing the peace of many streets with her
blasphemies and obscene language. And sometime ago-
I read in another column of the ‘“ Sad death of an old
Irishwoman.”” Mary Murphy had been pea-picking,
she had got drunk, she had gone to sleep in a vacant
rickyard, had apparently lighted her pipe sometime
during the night, and the morning found poor Mary
reduced more or less to a cinder. John Jones, with
whom she cohabited, was the principal witness at the
inquest. Both of them had got drunk out of their earn-
ings at pea-picking, both had gone to sleep in the rick-
yard, and when John Jones awoke next morning from
his drunken carouse, his companion had gone to her:
reward, and, let me hope, to a merciful judgment.

And I fell to thinking, Mr. Editor, of how many
Irish emigrants is Mary Murphy the type, or was her
miserable end typical of the ends of many of the men
and women who leave Ireland for a foreign country?
Was Mary ever a simple Irish peasant girl? Did she-
ever in her youth peep shyly and modestly at some-
Irish gorsoon? Or did she ever blush when some cen-
sorious neighbour remarked on her ‘‘ pulling a cord ”’
with some boy or another? And if she ever were, or
did, these things, how came that drunkenness, that co-
habitation, that forgetfulness of all things Catholic,
that miserable end of hers? Had she remained in
Ireland, would an Irish girl with the name of Mary
Murphy—Irish of the Irish—have developed into such
a human wreck as she became?

But here my musings were interrupted by a knock at
the door. When I answered the knock there stood a
winsome Irish girl. ' Was this Mrs. Lahy's’ (my
name is not Lahy, but that is neither here nor there).
‘“Yes,”” I said, ‘“this is Mrs. Lahy’s.”” And then
commenced a whirlwind of talk of which I could make

·on~ goes off in company with Greek, another with

Whi?h res_ults iu this, that a candidate is sharply put 
t? his choice between Greek and Gaelic. It is impos-
. ~1ble to take both together, and since this system obtains 
in the earlier examinations, needless to say, the habits 
there acquired are carried into the later ones as well. 
Hence th~ present system of the Royal University has 
in practice this effect that no student can study the 
literature of Greece or Rome, and that of his own 
-country, at the same time. I speak of the literature of 
Greece and Rome, because, as everyone knows, no 
really deep and satisfactory study of Latin _literature is 
possible, unless . combined with the study of Greek

The result is that the number of 
Gaelic students in the R.U .I. is far less than it would 
be in the natural course of things. 
• 
Now the fact that au Irish student should thus be 
put to his election between Classics and Irish is deplor-
able upon a number of grounds. 
First, it is only neces-
·sary to mention the names of Sedulius, Aileran, or Dicuil 
to recalJ how cherished was the study of the Classics 
by our forefathers. 
In the days of its intellectual 
·greatness, Ireland was the home of classical learning, 
,and it is, as we know, in the annotations by Irish monks 
upon classical texts that the oldest Irish is at pi"esent 
to be found . 
No divorce could, therefore, possibly be . 
more ungrateful, uuhis~orical or unnatural than that 
of classical and Gaelic learning, which the R.U.I. 
,system, probably through a mere oversight, at present 
efl'1:;cts. 
Nor need we go back to distant ages. . We 
still mourn the loss of tbe late Professor Strachan, 
whose scholarship was equally profound in Celtic and 
classical literature. 
H is melancholy to think that the 
revival of classical studies, recently inaugurated and 
•supported by such men as the Bishop of Limerick, will, 
if , it succeeds, unless conditions are changed, in the 
manner I suggest, hinder the ~tudy of Gaelic, and that, 
_ on the other hand, the Gaelic revival must have done a

. 
It is not however, merely on historical or sentimental 
grounds that one . must ;·egret , the se~erance of . Gaelic

rhe two stuches are emmeutly

But there is also an important scientific reason for

Latin and Irish are related very closely,

Enropean languages lliost nearly allied. For ph1lolog1cal

portance every day-a knowledge of either language is

Thus the separation of the study of Classics and

as the Royal University, amounts to a positive crime ,

Again, the comparison of Homeric

suggest that students taking Greek, who would be, m

standing the name, the st,udents do no experiments.

study

liternture

lege should devote their endeavours.
